findings.title: One-click purchase flow dependency on payment method storage
findings.description: The implementation of the one-click purchase flow (GROW-101) requires the ability to securely store tokenized payment credentials. This is a dependency that must be addressed before the feature can be developed.
assumptions: Tokenized payment credentials can be securely stored and retrieved.

evidence.excerpt: Requires storing tokenized payment credentials and skipping the review step for eligible users.

recommendations: Ensure secure storage mechanisms are in place for tokenized credentials.

findings.title: Checkout API latency impacting feature rollout
findings.description: The current checkout API latency exceeds the SLO, which is a critical issue (PLAT-201). This latency could hinder the performance of new features and negatively impact user experience.
assumptions: Optimizations can be made to reduce API latency.

evidence.excerpt: Current checkout API p95 latency is 1,200ms which exceeds our 500ms SLO.

recommendations: Prioritize API latency optimization before rolling out new features.

findings.title: Fix payment retry reliability before new features
findings.description: The payment retry reliability issue (PLAT-202) must be resolved to prevent double charges and maintain user trust. This is a critical blocking issue that must be addressed before implementing new checkout features.
assumptions: Fixing the retry mechanism will resolve the silent failure issue.

evidence.excerpt: Payment retries are failing silently in 3.8% of cases due to idempotency key collisions.

recommendations: Implement a fix for the payment retry mechanism as a priority.

findings.title: Migration to new payment gateway as a prerequisite
findings.description: The migration to the new payment gateway (PLAT-203) is planned to reduce transaction fees and improve support. This migration will block feature development during its duration and must be factored into the delivery timeline.
assumptions: Migration can be completed within the estimated 6-8 weeks.

evidence.excerpt: Migration to StripeConnect is planned to reduce transaction fees by 0.3%.

recommendations: Plan feature rollout post-migration to avoid delays.

findings.title: Mobile checkout crashes need urgent attention
findings.description: The checkout crashes on mobile devices (CUST-301) are critical and must be addressed to prevent user frustration and loss of sales. This issue is blocking further feature development.
assumptions: The crash issue can be replicated and fixed.

evidence.excerpt: Multiple users reporting that the checkout page crashes on iOS Safari and Android Chrome.

recommendations: Prioritize fixing mobile checkout crashes before new features.

findings.title: Auto-apply best coupon feature for V1
findings.description: The auto-apply best coupon feature (GROW-103) should be included in V1 as it is expected to reduce cart abandonment and improve user experience.
assumptions: The feature can be implemented without significant technical debt.

evidence.excerpt: Automatically detect and apply the best available coupon or discount code.

recommendations: Include auto-apply coupon feature in V1.

findings.title: Implement social proof badges in V2
findings.description: The social proof badges feature (GROW-102) can be included in V2 as it is less critical than other features and can be developed after addressing more pressing issues.
assumptions: The feature can be developed without impacting existing functionality.

evidence.excerpt: Display real-time purchase activity badges during checkout.

recommendations: Plan to implement social proof badges in V2.

summary: The analysis identifies critical dependencies and risks associated with the QuickPay Checkout Redesign. Key issues such as API latency, payment retry reliability, and mobile checkout crashes must be addressed in the MVP phase to ensure a stable platform before rolling out new features. The phased delivery plan suggests prioritizing essential features in MVP and V1, with additional enhancements planned for V2.
